body.text: An if statement runs a block of code only when a condition is true. An else block runs when it is false. This is how programs make decisions based on the current state.

body.desc: Run this block only when score is 10 or higher
Run this block when the condition is false

lines: // === checks value AND type — always use === not ==
// >= means greater than or equal to
// !  means NOT — !true is false, !false is true

body.label: === is not =
body.text: A single = puts a value into a variable. Triple === checks whether two values are equal. Using = inside an if condition is one of the most common bugs in JavaScript.

exercises.title: Write a decision
tasks: Declare a variable called temperature set to 22.
Write an if/else: log 'Wear a jacket' if temperature is below 15, otherwise log 'No jacket needed'.
Change temperature to 10 and run again — confirm the output changes.
Add a third condition using else if: if temperature is above 30, log 'It is very hot'.
Challenge: rewrite the logic as a function called getAdvice(temp) that returns the message instead of logging it.
exercises.hint: else if goes between if and else: if (...) { } else if (...) { } else { }

exercises.title: Debug the conditional
tasks: The function below should log 'You win!' when score reaches 5 — but it never does.
Find the bug. It is a single character error.
Fix it and confirm the win message appears.
Then change the target from 5 to 10 and test again.
exercises.hint: Look carefully at the comparison operator inside the if condition. = and === are very different.
